subject: english
chapter_title: Wisdom Paves the Way (Wit and Wisdom)

main_sections.title: Wisdom Paves the Way
main_sections.prose_summary: This two-scene play is set in Ujjain and follows four young men — Ram Datt, Shiv Datt, Har Datt, and Dev Datt — who have been travelling for ten days in search of employment and hope to gain an audience with the King. In Scene I on the road to Ujjain, the four men spot fresh camel tracks and each independently deduces a different physical characteristic of the animal from close observation alone: Ram Datt notices the camel is lame in one leg (one footprint fainter than the others); Shiv Datt deduces it is blind in the right eye (foliage eaten only on the left side of the road); Har Datt determines the tail is short (small droplets of blood from mosquito bites that a longer tail would have fended off); and Dev Datt concludes the camel has a stomach ailment (forefeet prints deeper than the hind foot, suggesting the animal draws up its hind legs in discomfort). When a distressed merchant runs up searching for his lost camel, the four men describe it accurately without having seen it. The merchant, incredulous and suspicious, accuses them of theft and drags them before the King. In Scene II at the King's Court, the King methodically questions each young man about his reasoning. Each explanation — drawn from careful observation of tracks, foliage, and physical signs on the road — impresses the King with its precision and logic. The King dismisses the merchant's baseless accusations, rebukes him for hasty judgement, and invites all four young men to become royal advisers, declaring that such observational wisdom is rare and highly valued. The court erupts in applause. The play celebrates the power of evidence-based reasoning, calm dignity under accusation, and the kind of practical wisdom that serves justice.

reading_for_comprehension.section_name: Let us read + Let us discuss + Let us think and reflect

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Complete the following sentences with suitable reasons. One example has been done for you.

question_bank.stem:
Complete with suitable reasons:
2. Dev Datt said, 'Indeed, we ought to be united in this hope. That is why we press on towards Ujjain,' because _______.
3. Har Datt said, 'Look there, friends. A camel has passed this way. The tracks are fresh,' because _______.
4. Shiv Datt said, 'It appears to be a merchant, running as if pursued by trouble,' because _______.
5. The merchant said, 'How could you possibly know that? The poor creature has been unwell for two weeks now. Surely you are a healer!' because _______.
6. Dev Datt said, 'You are free to do as you wish, sir. If the King desires our presence, we shall be waiting under that banyan tree yonder,' because _______.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Arrange the following events in order of their occurrence from Scene II of the play. One example has been done for you.

question_bank.stem:
Number these events in order of occurrence in Scene II:
1. The King makes the four young men his advisers, impressed by their skills. (8)
2. Ram Datt explains how he infers from the tracks that the camel is lame.
3. The King asks the merchant to clearly state his accusation.
4. The King praises the young men for their observations and dismisses the merchant's accusation.
5. Shiv Datt reveals that the camel is blind in its right eye as it had only chewed on the leaves on the left side of the road.
6. Dev Datt explains his observation about the camel's pain and careful movement.
7. Har Datt clarifies how he knew that the camel has a short tail.
8. The merchant accuses the four men of knowing a lot about his missing camel.

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
Read the given extracts and answer the questions that follow.

Extract 1: Dev Datt: Ten days are but a short span in the grand scheme of things... Ram Datt: If only we could secure an audience with the King of Ujjain. I am confident he would recognise our talents and appoint us to his service. Shiv Datt: I share your belief. Dev Datt: Indeed, we ought to be united in this hope. That is why we press on towards Ujjain. Har Datt: (Pausing thoughtfully) Gaining an audience with the King is no simple task. We need to devise a strategy.

question_bank.stem:
Identify the false statement from the two given below and rectify it:
A. Dev Datt believes that finding employment is an easy task.
B. Ram Datt is confident that the King would recognise their talents.
What does Ram Datt's confidence in gaining the King's recognition tell us about him?

In the line, 'That's why we press on towards Ujjain', the phrase 'press on' refers to __________.
A. walking with heavy steps
B. moving forward with determination
C. slowing down frequently for rest
D. stopping briefly to save time

Har Datt says, 'We need to devise a strategy.' What can be inferred about Har Datt's character?
A. He is doubtful of their abilities and wants to give up.
B. He is eager to rush into the palace without preparation.
C. He is cautious and thoughtful, preferring to plan ahead.
D. He relies on others to make decisions for the group.
options: He is doubtful of their abilities and wants to give up.
He is eager to rush into the palace without preparation.
He is cautious and thoughtful, preferring to plan ahead.
He relies on others to make decisions for the group.

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
Read the given extracts and answer the questions that follow.

Extract 2: King: (To the four men) You have demonstrated remarkable intelligence and the ability to see beyond the obvious. Such minds are rare and highly valued in my court. I extend to you the honour of becoming my advisers. Your counsel shall guide my decisions, and your wisdom shall be a beacon for my kingdom. What do you say to my offer? Ram Datt: (Stepping forward) Your Highness, it is an honour we had not dared to dream of.

question_bank.stem:
Select the option that is true for both Assertion (A) and Reason (R):
(A): The Merchant left the court feeling humbled and ashamed.
(R): The King commanded that the four wise men join his court.
A. Both (A) and (R) are true, and (R) is the correct explanation of (A).
B. Both (A) and (R) are true, but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A).
C. (A) is true, but (R) is false.
D. (A) is false, but (R) is true.
options: Both (A) and (R) are true, and (R) is the correct explanation of (A).
Both (A) and (R) are true, but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A).

question_bank.stem:
The line 'See beyond the obvious' means that the men had the ability to ______.
A. challenge common beliefs with reasoning
B. interpret information in traditional ways
C. notice details that others might overlook
D. predict what is going to happen in the future
options: notice details that others might overlook
predict what is going to happen in the future

question_bank.stem: Ram Datt described the King's offer as 'an honour we had not dared to dream of' because ___________.
How does the King's offer to the four men reflect his values as a ruler?
What does the dusty road leading to Ujjain tell us about the challenges of the young men's journey?
Why did the merchant become suspicious of the four men? What does this tell us about him?
How do the four young men's observations about the camel serve as a turning point in the play?
Why did the King believe the four young men and not the merchant?
How does the capability of the four young men make them suitable to become advisors?
Why does the merchant feel ashamed for accusing the four young men of stealing his camel? Explain in your own words.

listening.transcript_text:
SPEAKER (i): I love witty characters because they make serious situations funny! Even when things seem tense, they always have a clever response. Such characters never panic, think fast, and solve problems in the smartest way. I wish I could think that quickly!
SPEAKER (ii): Witty characters are the best because they make stories exciting. I especially like how they turn ordinary moments into something memorable. It makes me feel like intelligence and humour are just as powerful as strength.
SPEAKER (iii): I enjoy witty characters because they always have an answer for everything! They use humour instead of fighting, and that's really wonderful. Even in folktales, they prove that smart thinking can win over force. It makes me laugh but also teaches me to stay calm in tricky situations.
SPEAKER (iv): Witty characters add charm to a story. Their intelligence keeps the reader engaged, and their humour makes learning fun. Whether it's a folktale or a modern one, a clever character always stands out. I often feel that wit and wisdom go hand-in-hand!

tasks_verbatim.task_text: You will listen to four people sharing their thoughts on why they enjoy witty characters in stories. As you listen, match statements 1–6 given below to the speakers (i)–(iv). There are two statements you do not need.

question_bank.stem:
Match each statement to the speaker (i)–(iv). Two statements are not needed:
1. Witty characters prove that cleverness is as powerful as strength.
2. Witty characters show us ways to make learning enjoyable.
3. Witty characters in a story keep the reader involved.
4. Witty characters teach me that tricky situations can be managed calmly.
5. Witty characters add excitement to a story by their extraordinary deeds.
6. Witty characters can deactivate tense situations with their cleverness.

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
The way in which a word is stressed in a sentence can change its meaning. Work in pairs. Take turns to say the following sentences aloud by stressing on the highlighted words. Share the meanings of the sentences with each other and teacher.
• I take my dog for a walk in the evening.
• I take my dog for a walk in the evening.
• I take my dog for a walk in the evening.
• I take my dog for a walk in the evening.
• I take my dog for a walk in the evening.

question_bank.stem: Say each version of 'I take my dog for a walk in the evening' stressing the highlighted word. Explain how the meaning changes in each case.

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
Statements and exclamatory sentences usually end with a falling tone. Practise by saying the following sentences with the correct intonation:
• Good day, kind sirs.
• We regret to inform you that we do not know where it is.
• You describe it perfectly!
• You shall come with me to face the King!

question_bank.stem:
Practise saying the following sentences with falling intonation:
1. Good day, kind sirs.
2. We regret to inform you that we do not know where it is.
3. You describe it perfectly!
4. You shall come with me to face the King!

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
You have observed that the kutcha road leading to your colony is in a very bad condition due to heavy rains. The potholes are dangerous for people and can lead to accidents. They also serve as a breeding ground for mosquitoes. Write a letter of complaint to the Commissioner, Municipal Corporation or the Sarpanch, Gram Panchayat to look into the matter and take necessary action. Follow the format of a formal letter:
Paragraph 1: state purpose and issue
Paragraph 2: give details and consequences
Paragraph 3: give suggestions and request for swift action

question_bank.stem: Write a formal letter of complaint to the Commissioner/Sarpanch about a damaged kutcha road in your colony. Include: purpose (para 1), details and consequences (para 2), suggestions and request (para 3).

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
Complete the sentences by filling in the noun form of the textual words given in the brackets.
1. Some word games need a lot of thought and _________________. (deduce)
2. We need to make close _________________ of animals in order to understand their behaviour. (observe)
3. A detailed _________________ of the documents shows a lot of errors. (scrutinise)
4. You should not make any false _________________ against anyone. (accuse)
5. She was given an award in _________________ of her contribution to literature. (recognise)

question_bank.stem:
Fill in the noun form:
1. thought and _________ (deduce)
2. close _________ of animals (observe)
3. detailed _________ of documents (scrutinise)
4. false _________ against anyone (accuse)
5. award in _________ of her contribution (recognise)

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
Match the expressions in Column 1 with their meanings in Column 2.
Column 1: 1. with all our hearts 2. to be precise 3. gain an audience with 4. catch his breath 5. erupts in applause 6. grand scheme of things 7. taken aback 8. more than meets the eye
Column 2: (i) surprised (ii) starts cheering together (iii) hidden information that is not obvious (iv) exact and accurate (v) meeting or hearing someone (vi) complete sincerity (vii) to pause or rest for some time (viii) overall plan of life in a larger context

question_bank.stem:
Match expressions (1–8) with meanings (i–viii):
1. with all our hearts
2. to be precise
3. gain an audience with
4. catch his breath
5. erupts in applause
6. grand scheme of things
7. taken aback
8. more than meets the eye
Now, make sentences using any five of the expressions from Column 1.

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
Match the different meanings of 'face' and 'bear' with the correct usage in the sentences given in the table below. (n. = noun, v. = verb)
face: 1. (n.) front part of head from forehead to chin 2. (v.) to be positioned with face towards 3. (v.) to deal with a difficult task 4. (n.) front or surface of a thing
bear: 1. (v.) tolerate 2. (v.) accept 3. (n.) a large heavy mammal 4. (v.) produce; give birth to

question_bank.stem:
Match meanings of 'face' (1–4) with sentences (i–iv):
(i) Ravi turned to face his father. (ii) The face of the clock was painted red and blue. (iii) She has a round, smiling face. (iv) We should be ready to face life's challenges.

Match meanings of 'bear' (1–4) with sentences (i–iv):
(i) The trees took a long time to bear fruit. (ii) I spotted a bear on a wildlife safari. (iii) Riya agreed to bear the responsibilities with confidence. (iv) I can't bear the sound of vehicles honking.

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
Match the highlighted modal verbs in the sentences in Column 1 with the functions they express in Column 2.
Column 1: 1. I will gladly pay your fee if you can cure it. 2. You must have stolen it and sold it off. 3. You shall come with me to face the King! 4. How could you possibly know that? 5. I am confident he would recognise our talents. 6. A camel with a long tail would have fended off these pests. 7. They may tell us more than meets the eye. 8. May your wisdom shine upon Ujjain. 9. Indeed, we ought to be united in this hope. 10. We need to devise a strategy. 11. You speak of my camel as if it stood before you, yet you dare to claim not to have seen it!
Column 2: (i) expression of disbelief (ii) imagined (hypothetical) possibility (iii) moral obligation or advice (iv) challenge (v) logical conclusion (vi) wish or hope (vii) necessity (viii) prediction (ix) willingness (x) command (xi) possibility

question_bank.stem: Match modal verbs in sentences 1–11 with functions (i)–(xi).

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
Complete the following story with modal verbs from the box (may, must, should, can, might, could, need to) using the clues from the functions given within brackets.

An old man, known for his wisdom, lived in a small village. One day, a young boy asked him, 'How 1. _______ (ability) I become wise like you?' The old man smiled and said, 'Wisdom 2. _______ (necessity) be earned through patience. You 3. _______ (suggestion) listen, more than you speak.' The boy thought for a while and replied, 'It 4. _______ (weak possibility) take years, but I'll try.' The old man nodded. 'You 5. _______ (obligation) learn from your mistakes too. Wisdom 6. _______ (possibility) come slowly, but it always arrives for those who seek it.' The boy knew he 7. _______ (ability) become wise if he followed the old man's advice.

question_bank.stem:
Fill in blanks 1–7 with appropriate modal verbs from the box (may, must, should, can, might, could, need to) using the function clues:
1. How _______ (ability) I become wise?
2. Wisdom _______ (necessity) be earned.
3. You _______ (suggestion) listen more.
4. It _______ (weak possibility) take years.
5. You _______ (obligation) learn from mistakes.
6. Wisdom _______ (possibility) come slowly.
7. He _______ (ability) become wise.

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
Rewrite the following sentences using appropriate modal verbs. You may take clues from the highlighted words. One example has been done for you.
1. It is advisable for you to think before you speak. → You should think before you speak.
2. It is unnecessary to worry about things beyond your control.
3. It is not possible for a person to gain experience without taking risks.
4. It is essential to treat others with kindness, even when they disagree with you.
5. It is not allowed to interrupt when someone else is speaking.

question_bank.stem:
Rewrite sentences 2–5 using appropriate modal verbs:
2. It is unnecessary to worry about things beyond your control.
3. It is not possible for a person to gain experience without taking risks.
4. It is essential to treat others with kindness.
5. It is not allowed to interrupt when someone else is speaking.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Staging a play requires creativity, organisation, and teamwork. Follow the 13-step process given in the textbook (choosing a play, assigning roles, auditions, script reading, stage planning, set design, costumes, makeup, light/sound, dress rehearsal, promotion, backstage organisation, performance). Now, present the enactment of the play 'Wisdom Paves the Way'. Ensure you use the correct intonation and sentence stress.

question_bank.stem: Stage the play 'Wisdom Paves the Way' following the 13 steps provided. Use correct intonation and sentence stress in performance.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Stories related to wit and wisdom have always fascinated readers. Go to the library and read stories associated with the following: Vikramaditya and Betal, Tenali Rama, Gopal the Jester, Panchatantra Tales.

question_bank.stem: Read at least one story from: Vikramaditya and Betal, Tenali Rama, Gopal the Jester, or Panchatantra Tales. Share a summary with your classmates.
